coredata.dc:description: Accidental leakage of uranium causes a huge threat to human health and environmental security. Herein, a novel fluorescent MOF-on-MOF hydrogel complex, namely Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM, is constructed using a “three-in-one” synthetic strategy for the simultaneous adsorption enrichment and sensitive detection of uranyl ions (UO2 2+) in water. Combining ZIF-8 with Tb-MOF enables the composite to exhibit remarkable fluorescence enhancement, large fluorescence quantum yield, and exceptional stability. When used to detect of UO2 2+ in water, the method demonstrates excellent sensitivity (LOD = 0.551 nmol/L). Experimental characterizations suggest that the static quenching effect (SQE) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) may be possible sensing mechanisms. Furthermore, Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM is rich in amino and carboxyl groups and exhibits superior hydrophilicity, an abundant pore structure, and numerous active adsorption sites, which leads to a remarkable absorption capability of 603 mg/g specifically for UO2 2+. Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM has a straightforward recycling strategy, and its adsorption efficiency remains above 80 % after 4 cycles of use. Thus, this research introduces an innovative approach for synthesis of a low-cost and multifunctional adsorbent MOF-on-MOF hydrogel composite, which can be employed for the simultaneous adsorption and detection of UO2 2+.

head.ce:title: ZIF-8-induced fluorescence-enhanced multifunctional MOF-on-MOF hydrogels for efficient adsorption and sensitive detection of uranyl ions in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Public Health, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang, Hunan, 421001, People's Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accidental leakage of uranium causes a huge threat to human health and environmental security. Herein, a novel fluorescent MOF-on-MOF hydrogel complex, namely Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM, is constructed using a “three-in-one” synthetic strategy for the simultaneous adsorption enrichment and sensitive detection of uranyl ions (UO2 2+) in water. Combining ZIF-8 with Tb-MOF enables the composite to exhibit remarkable fluorescence enhancement, large fluorescence quantum yield, and exceptional stability. When used to detect of UO2 2+ in water, the method demonstrates excellent sensitivity (LOD = 0.551 nmol/L). Experimental characterizations suggest that the static quenching effect (SQE) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) may be possible sensing mechanisms. Furthermore, Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM is rich in amino and carboxyl groups and exhibits superior hydrophilicity, an abundant pore structure, and numerous active adsorption sites, which leads to a remarkable absorption capability of 603 mg/g specifically for UO2 2+. Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM has a straightforward recycling strategy, and its adsorption efficiency remains above 80 % after 4 cycles of use. Thus, this research introduces an innovative approach for synthesis of a low-cost and multifunctional adsorbent MOF-on-MOF hydrogel composite, which can be employed for the simultaneous adsorption and detection of UO2 2+.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the worldwide need for eco-friendly and renewable energy sources escalates, nuclear energy is becoming increasingly important as a clean and efficient form of energy [1,2]. The rapid development of the nuclear energy industry is also accompanied with a series of environmental risks, such as pollution occurs during mineral extraction, inadequate management of radioactive by-products, and sporadic incidents at nuclear facilities, which may result in the release of the radioactive element uranium into the ecosystem, resulting in enduring environmental contamination and presenting a grave risk to human [3–5]. Among oxidation states of uranium, UO2 2+ has been the focus of attention for its high chemical stability and solubility. UO2 2+ is not only easy to migrate freely in water but also persists due to its long half-life, which increases the difficulty of environmental management [6,7]. More seriously, UO2 2+ exhibits the dual threat of radiotoxicity and heavy metal toxicity to the human body, and prolonged exposure may lead to serious consequences such as kidney damage, urinary tract diseases, and even lung cancer [8–10]. Given the context above, the quest for efficient and innovative materials, as well as the creation of straightforward and swift novel methods for the effective adsorption and real-time monitoring of UO2 2+ in the environment, presents significant implications for alleviating the adverse effects of radioactive contamination on ecosystems.

Currently, the primary detection modalities employed for UO2 2+ encompass inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [11], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [12], and flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS) [13], which are highly sensitive and accurate. Concurrent with the detection methodologies for UO2 2+, academics also actively explore techniques for the removal and separation of UO2 2+ from environmental matrices, which include adsorptive separation [9,14], membrane filtration [15], chemical precipitation method [16], photocatalytic degradation [4], and biological treatment [3]. However, these methods only focus on the adsorption or detection of UO2 2+ alone. A multifunctional new method integrated with adsorption and detection is able to monitor the concentration of UO2 2+ in real time while adsorbing it, provide timely feedback on the adsorption effect, and also effectively enrich UO2 2+ in the water through adsorption, thus simplifying the sample pre-treatment process and improving the detection accuracy. Therefore, developing a simple and convenient new method for the simultaneous adsorption and detection of UO2 2+ is an attractive research direction.

As an emerging branch in the field of MOF, MOF-on-MOF hybrid materials are made by combining two or more MOF units in a conjugated manner. This integration significantly enhances the physical properties of the material, encompassing pore structure and surface properties, while retaining all the beneficial traits of individual MOFs and attaining multifaceted functional synergies, surpassing the capabilities of a solitary MOF [17]. For example, Xin's research group synthesized F-10c ⊂ ZIF-8-on-In-dpda luminescent sensors through a stepwise homologous growth method involving the encapsulation of multiple guests, which enabled the highly sensitive detection of histamine and a spectrum of fluorescence color adjustments [18].

In this work, MOF-on-MOF polyacrylamide hydrogel complexes (Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM) are prepared for the adsorption and detection of UO2 2+ using an efficient microwave synthesis method, liquid phase epitaxy (LPE), and simple covalent cross-linking. Tb-MOF prepared from the lanthanide metal Tb3+ and 2,4,6-tris((p-carboxyphenyl)amino)1,3,5-triazine (H3TATAB) possesses favorable fluorescence properties, such as sharp rocket-like emission peaks, large Stokes shifts, and long fluorescence lifetimes. Zeolite imidazolium salt framework-8 (ZIF-8) holds an extensive particular surface area and good hydrophilicity. The Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite not only inherits the beneficial properties of both constituent materials but also exhibits enhanced thermal stability, improved fluorescence characteristics, and an increased adsorption capacity. The metal ion Zn2+ in ZIF-8, as a transition metal, can effectively promote the efficiency of energy transfer from the ligand to the lanthanide metal, facilitated by the “whole antenna” phenomenon, enhancing the fluorescence performance of the material. To facilitate recycling and prevent secondary contamination, Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 powder is encapsulated into a hydrogel to form a Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM complex, and the amine-rich environment of the PAM matrix significantly boosts the adsorption efficiency of UO2 2+.

When Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM is placed in water samples containing UO2 2+, functional groups that incorporate amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups in this material effectively capture and bind the UO2 2+, achieving the high-efficiency enrichment of UO2 2+. Distinctively, the fluorescence of the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM complexes is selectively diminished upon UO2 2+ adsorption due to a reaction involving static quenching effect (SQE) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanisms. There is a good linear relationship between the UO2 2+ concentration and the rate of fluorescence quenching. Accordingly, we have pioneered a new method with both adsorption and detection functions for UO2 2+ in water. The method exhibits a low detection limit and large adsorption capacity and facilitates subsequent recycling, demonstrating significant advantages (Scheme 1 ).
The supporting information included thorough descriptions of the experimental reagents, instruments, and procedures used to assess detection and adsorption performance.

A solution containing 90 mg of Tb(NO3)3·6H2O and 50 mg of H3TATAB was formulated in a 10 mL mixture of H2O and DMF, with a volume ratio of 2:3. This mixture was vigorously stirred and then subjected to microwave irradiation at 140 °C for 4 h. Once the reaction was finished and the mixture had cooled down to ambient temperature, the residue was washed repeatedly with a sequence of DMF and methanol (2–3 cycles) to eliminate any unwanted byproducts. Finally, the purified residue was dehydrated in a vacuum desiccator at a temperature of 70 °C, yielding a pristine white powder form of Tb-MOF.

Initially, dried Tb-MOF powder was introduced into a 40 mL methanol mixture with 1.3 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and mixed continuously for 4 h. Following this, the supernatant liquid was separated via centrifugation, and the resulting precipitate was rinsed twice with methanol. Then, the precipitate was introduced into another 40 mL methanol mixture containing 4 g of 2-methylimidazole and mixed for an additional 4 h. Once the mixing was complete, the precipitate was isolated and rinsed twice more with methanol to eliminate any surplus 2-methylimidazole and methanol. The precipitate, now Tb-MOF@ZIF-8, was reintroduced into a methanol mixture with Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and mixed for another 4 h. Finally, the treated and washed Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 precipitate was cleaned with methanol and dried in a vacuum oven at 70 °C to remove all remaining solvents. The end product was a pure white powder of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8.

We precisely measured and combined 3 mg of the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite, 0.02 g of AM, and 0.004 g of MBA in a 3 mL volume of ultrapure water. The resulting suspension was sonicated for 5 min to ensure complete dissolution and uniform mixing of the components. Following this, 20 μL of a 25 % (v/v) GA solution and 0.02 g of APS were introduced into the well-mixed solution with precision. Vigorous stirring was applied once more to ensure uniform dispersion of the added substances. The mixture was then gently cast into a 2 × 7 cm mold, taking care to minimize air entrapment. The filled molds were left undisturbed for 2 h to facilitate the initial setting of the solution. Subsequently, the entire mold was moved to a refrigerated environment to undergo overnight curing, thereby finalizing the fabrication of the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM.

The structural properties of the composites ZIF-8, Tb-MOF, Tb-MOF@ZIF-8, and Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1 a illustrated that the ZIF-8 nanoparticles exhibited a regular ortho-polyhedral structure with uniform distribution. Tb-MOF nanomaterials showed an irregular rod-like structure and aggregated into clusters (Fig. 1b). Its smooth surface properties implied that Tb-MOF could be a potential seed for further epitaxial growth [19]. As shown in Fig. 1c, when ZIF-8 was grown in situ on the surface of Tb-MOF, the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite presented an irregular polyhedral shape and aggregated into clusters. TEM images (Fig. 1d) further confirmed the growth of ZIF-8 nanoplates around the Tb-MOF nanorods to form a compact composite structure. Fig. 1g's mapping data revealed the uniform distribution of the elements C, N, Zn, and Tb across the surface of the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite. When Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 was further combined with PAM to form a hydrogel, Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM exhibited a 3D porous mesh structure (Fig. 1e). This structure provided abundant adsorption sites. Fig. 1f showed a local magnified image of the hydrogel network, and the uniformly distributed fine particles (shown as red circles) on its network structure were observed, which were the embodiment of the successful loading of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite into the hydrogel network.

The chemical composition and structure of the composites, namely ZIF-8, Tb-MOF, Tb-MOF@ZIF-8, and Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM, were revealed through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the characteristic peaks of Tb-MOF were predominantly observed at 862.5 cm−1 (assigned to 1,3,5-triazine), 1242.3 cm−1 (attributable to C–N stretching vibration), 1487.3 cm−1, and 1596.6 cm−1 (corresponding to the stretching vibration of the aromatic ring skeleton). These peaks confirmed the successful synthesis of Tb-MOF [20]. The distinctive peaks indicative of ZIF-8 were observed at 424.2 cm−1 (attributable to Zn–N stretching), 690.1 cm−1, and 756.9 cm−1 (reflecting Zn–N and Zn–O vibrations), alongside peaks at 1149.4 cm−1 and 1579.4 cm−1 (associated with imidazole toroidal stretching and bending modes), and peaks at 2931.6 cm−1 and 3138.5 cm−1 (corresponding to C–H stretching). These peaks collectively confirmed the successful synthesis of ZIF-8 [21]. The FT-IR patterns of the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composites showed the characteristic peaks of both Tb-MOF and ZIF-8, indicated that the two had been successfully combined to form the composite. For the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM composite, new characteristic peaks such as 1415.4 cm−1 (C–N stretching vibration), 3182.7 cm−1 and 3347.5 cm−1 (N–H and O–H stretching vibration) manifested in the FT-IR pattern [21]. In contrast, the characteristic peaks of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 were no longer obvious, probably because Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 was encapsulated into the hydrogel.

X-ray diffraction (XRD), as a crystal detection method, provided information on the crystal structure of materials. Fig. 2b illustrated that the XRD patterns for all materials demonstrated high crystalline quality. The diffraction peaks of Tb-MOF at positions 9.87, 15.26, and 19.98°, were in good agreement with the modeled Tb-MOF, suggesting the successful synthesis of Tb-MOF [20]. ZIF-8's diffraction peaks located at 7.39, 10.41, 12.74, and 18.04° corresponded well with the expected standard diffraction pattern [21]. The XRD pattern of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 showed diffraction peaks of both Tb-MOF and ZIF-8 with clear peak shapes and moderate intensities, confirmed the successful synthesis of the heterostructure Tb-MOF@ZIF-8. However, similar diffraction peaks of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 did not appear in the XRD pattern of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM possibly because Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 was encapsulated in an amorphous hydrogel.

Zeta potentials of ZIF-8, Tb-MOF, and the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite were measured and the results were in Fig. S1. The ZIF-8, Tb-MOF, and Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 all exhibited negative charge characteristics with Zeta potentials of −4.88 mV, −16.4 mV, and −20.5 mV, respectively. This might be related to the fact that the surfaces of these materials were rich in negatively charged groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, or uncoordinated nitrogen atoms, which readily dissociate in water and produce negative charges. Notably, the absolute value of the Zeta potential of the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite was significantly larger than that of ZIF-8 and Tb-MOF alone. Given that UO2 2+ typically bear a positive charge in water, materials with a negative surface charge were more prone to attract and adsorb UO2 2+ through electrostatic forces.

The stability performance of the material determined its suitability for practical applications. The material's thermal, salt, and fluorescence stability were evaluated. Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) was utilized to assess the thermal stability of the materials. As shown in Fig. S2, Tb-MOF, Tb-MOF@ZIF-8, and Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM all exhibited good thermal stability below 200 °C. Fig. S2a illustrated the thermogravimetric analysis curve for Tb-MOF, showed a primary weight loss interval between 315 and 800 °C associated with the elimination of DMF and the collapse of the structural framework. The primary weight loss phase for Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 was observed within the temperature range of 410–800 °C (as depicted in Fig. S2b), resulted in a cumulative mass loss of 40 %. Notably, the collapse of the organic framework of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 started at 410 °C, demonstrated an enhancement in thermal stability when compared to the individual Tb-MOF, which started to decompose at 315 °C. This demonstrated the MOF-on-MOF mode's superiority in improving the material's thermal stability. The introduction of PAM reduced the overall thermal stability of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM. However, as shown in Fig. S2c, despite a 70 % mass loss at 410 °C, Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM still maintained commendable thermal stability below 200 °C. The fluorescence stability of the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM was evaluated, with the outcomes depicted in Fig. S3a. After one week of storage at ambient temperature, the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged for the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM composite, which indicated that Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM possessed good fluorescence stability and was suitable for use in practical conditions. Then, the salt resistance of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM was evaluated. As depicted in Fig. S3b, the fluorescence intensity of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM exhibited remarkable stability, with no significant decrement, even in the presence of NaCl at a concentration as high as 1 mol/L. This result fully proved that Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM had excellent salt resistance and could keep its optical properties stable in high-salinity environments, which was highly important for managing uranium tailings wastewater containing high concentrations of salt.

Fig. S4 displayed the UV–vis absorption spectra for ZIF-8, Tb-MOF, and the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite, with the peak absorption at 307 nm for Tb-MOF being due to π-π∗ or n-π∗ transitions within the aromatic conjugated sp2 domain [22]. ZIF-8 did not have UV–vis absorption peaks, but when Tb-MOF and ZIF-8 were combined to form Tb-MOF@ZIF-8, the UV–vis absorption profile of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 showed a change in peak shape from a broad peak to a sharp narrow peak and a blueshift in peak position compared to Tb-MOF alone, which proved that Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 was a new material distinguished from ZIF-8 and Tb-MOF.

Subsequently, we thoroughly investigated the fluorescence properties of these materials. As shown in Fig. 3 a, ZIF-8 and PAM were non-fluorescent materials, whereas Tb-MOF exhibited characteristic fluorescence emission peaks at 491, 547, 587, and 623 nm, which corresponded to the 5D4→7F6, 5D4→7F5, 5D4→7F4, and 5D4→7F3 for the 4f-4f jump. Under identical experimental settings, the emission peak wavelength of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 was consistent with that of Tb-MOF, but the fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced that as strong as 2.7 times of Tb-MOF. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yields were compared, revealing that the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite exhibited a quantum yield of 87 % in the aqueous phase, which is 3.95 times higher than that of the standalone Tb-MOF (22 %). (Fig. S5). Additionally, the luminescence properties of ZIF-8, Tb-MOF, and the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite were visually assessed. As illustrated in Fig. S6a, under 365 nm irradiation, the ZIF-8 solution exhibited no detectable fluorescence. In contrast, both Tb-MOF and the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite displayed distinct green luminescence. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite was significantly enhanced compared to that of the standalone Tb-MOF. We plotted chromaticity diagrams of the materials and calculated the CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates. The results showed that the coordinates were (0.28, 0.54) for Tb-MOF and (0.26, 0.56) for Tb-MOF@ZIF-8, and both of them exhibited green emission hue (Fig. S7), which was in agreement with the results of the emission spectra of the materials. Next, the fluorescence profiles of Tb-MOF@PAM and Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM were scanned (Fig. S6b). The positions of the fluorescence peaks of Tb-MOF@PAM and Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM were found to be unchanged, but the peak shapes became sharper, and the fluorescence intensity was reduced (Fig. 3a).

Further, to better understand the fluorescence enhancement behavior above, we plotted the Jablonsky energy level diagrams of Tb-MOF versus Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 (Fig. 3b). The luminescence behavior of Tb-MOF was primarily attributed to the absorption of energy by the organic ligand, 2,4,6-tris[(p-carboxyphenyl)amino]-1,3,5-triazine (H3TATAB), followed by efficient energy transfer to Tb3+. This energy was subsequently relayed to the emitting state of Tb3+, sensitizing its luminescence — a phenomenon commonly referred to as the “antenna effect”. The performance of the antenna effect depended on the ligand's conversion efficiency from a single-line state to a triple-line state, as well as the stability of the triple-line state. When ZIF-8 bound to Tb-MOF, Zn2+ as a transition element forms an l-chelate ligand structure with the organic ligand, which effectively restricted the ligand rotation and reduced non-radiative leaps, as well as stabilized the triply excited state of the ligand through the spin-orbit coupling effect. These changes enhanced the electron transfer efficiency and the occurrence of intersystem scrambling in Tb3+, which ultimately significantly enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the composite [23,24].

The sensing conditions (pH and reaction time) were optimized for the sensitive detection of UO2 2+. As shown in Fig. S8a, the influence of varying pH on the sensing effect was examined. At pH 6, Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM showed the strongest fluorescence intensity, and its fluorescence quench was most obvious after the reaction with UO2 2+. In addition, the time response curve showed that the fluorescence intensity of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM decreased as time went by but remained at the same value after 100 min (Fig. S8b).

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the luminescence intensity of the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM composite progressively diminished alongside rising UO2 2+ in the concentration range of 1–200 nmol/L. A good linear correlation was observed between the UO2 2+ concentration and the fluorescence quenching efficiency, calculated as (I 0–I)/I 0 (y = 0.00145x+0.07895, R
2 = 0.996) (Fig. 4b). The limit of detection was determined to be 0.551 nmol/L, according to the calculation of Equation (3) σ/k (σ represented the standard deviation obtained from the blank sample measurements of the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM composite, and k was the slope of the fluorescence intensity curve of the concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity of UO2 2+), which value significantly undercut the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's threshold for permissible UO2 2+ levels in potable water, which stood at 130 nmol/L [25].

Eighteen metal ions, included Zn2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Na+, Ag+, Cu2+, K+, Pb2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Sr2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, and V5+, and six anions, included NO3 −, Cl−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, I−, Ac−, and Br− were selected as potential interferences to investigate the selectivity of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM to UO2 2+. Except for Fe3+ (500 nmol/L), the concentration of metal cations was 1000 nmol/L, the concentration of anions was 100 μmol/L and the concentration of UO2 2+ was 200 nmol/L. The results were shown in Fig. 4c and d, where only UO2 2+ caused significant fluorescence quenching of the material. In contrast, the other common metal cations and anions in the water showed only slight and negligible fluorescence quenching. Actual aqueous environments usually contained mixtures of various ions. Therefore, an anti-interference experiment in identifying UO2 2+ from a mixed system was of practical interest. As shown in Fig. S9, in the three systems of mixed cations, mixed anions, and mixed ions, the material hardly underwent quenching. When UO2 2+ coexisted with the above system, the fluorescence of the system underwent a significant quenching phenomenon. This showed that the method had good selectivity and anti-interference ability for UO2 2+.

Actual samples, including river water, tap water, and tailings wastewater, were selected to assess the practicality of the proposed method. As shown in Table 1 , the results of UO2 2+ detection in river water, tap water, and tailings wastewater by this method were in good agreement with ICP-MS, and there was no statistically significant difference between them (P > 0.05) (Fig. S10). The recoveries were found to be between 94 % and 106 %, with the relative standard deviation (RSD) not exceeding 10.4 %. These findings suggested that the detection of UO2 2+ based on the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM was a very promising approach.

In general, fluorescence quenching was mainly attributed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), internal filtering effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and static quenching effect (SQE) [26,27]. To gain a deeper comprehension of the behavior associated with the fluorescence quenching by UO2 2+ on Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM, a series of experimental characterizations, such as Zeta potential analysis, fluorescence lifetime determination, cyclic voltammetry, solid-state UV spectroscopy, and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, were carried out.

As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of UO2 2+ did not intersect with the excitation and emission peaks of the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM composite material, whereby FRET and IFE were ruled out as the quenching mechanism. Further, fluorescence lifetime measurements (Fig. 5b) showed that the fluorescence lifetime of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 remained essentially unchanged in the presence of UO2 2+, the fluorescence quenching effect induced by UO2 2+ could likely be attributed to a static quenching event as a result of the formation of a complex between UO2 2+ and the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite [26]. As shown in Fig. 5c, the addition of UO2 2+ led to a redshift in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8. The peak shape and position of its fluorescence spectrum did not change when UO2 2+ reacted with Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 (Fig. S11). Still, its fluorescence intensity decreased. The results of the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra indicated the formation of non-fluorescent or weakly fluorescent ground-state complexes [28]. The SQE mechanism was verified by the standard Stern-Volmer plot (Equation (1)).
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
k
s v
[ Q ]
In Equation 1, F 0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 at 547 nm in the absence and presence of UO2 2+, respectively, K SV was the quenching constant, and Q was the molar concentration of UO2 2+. The Stern-Volmer plots (Fig. 5e) at different temperatures (20 °C, 40 °C, and 60 °C) showed good linear relationships, and the quenching constant decreased with increasing temperature and reached a maximum at 20 °C (2.48 × 10−6 L/mol), suggested that SQE was one of the possible quenching mechanisms.

Zeta potential analysis (Fig. 5d) showed that Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 was negatively charged (−20.54), while UO2 2+ was positively charged (8.66), and the Zeta potential of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8, after adsorbing UO2 2+, decreased to −14.44. This suggested a possible electrostatic interaction between UO2 2+ and the material.

To explore the PET mechanism, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LOMO) energy levels of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 were calculated based on the corresponding empirical formulae [29]. The empirical formulae were as follows:
(2)

= ‐ e
(
E ox
+ 4.4
)
(3)
E LOMO
= ‐ e
(
E red
+ 4.4
)
(4)

E LOMO
‐
E g

E ox and E red represented the initial points of the oxidation and reduction potentials, each, while E g signified the optical band gap energy. From the cyclic voltammogram of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 (Fig. 5f), the determination of the E red for the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite was found to be −0.75 eV. The E LOMO of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 was calculated to be −3.65 eV according to Equation (3). Due to the irreversible nature of the oxidation behavior, we were unable to obtain the energy value of E ox directly. However, the band gap (E g) of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 could be obtained from the solid-state UV absorption pattern. We could derive the E g of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 as 3.45 eV from the Tauc plot (Fig. 5g), and then the E HOMO of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 was calculated to be −7.10 eV according to Equation (4). Combined with the energy level data of UO2 2+ (−7.0 eV for E HOMO and −4.24 eV for E LOMO) [30], it could be inferred that when Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 was irradiated by the 330 nm excitation light, the electrons leapt from E HOMO to E LOMO to form the excited state. Since the E LOMO of UO2 2+ was lower than that of the material, which thermodynamically allowed electron transfer, the excited state electrons of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 were more inclined to transfer to UO2 2+. This prevented the electrons from going back to the E HOMO of the material, inhibited the fluorescence emission of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 (Fig. 5h), and resulted in quenching of the fluorescence of the material. To encapsulate, the mechanism of fluorescence detection of UO2 2+ by Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 was mainly attributed to the combined effect of SQE, PET, and electrostatic interaction.

First, we investigated the effects of temperature, pH, ionic strength, and coexisting ions on the adsorption of UO2 2+ by the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM. As shown in Fig. S12a, there was a maximum adsorption of UO2 2+ when the temperature was 30 °C. Fig. S12b showed the effect of adsorption capacity of the material on UO2 2+ at different pH. When the pH was in the range of 5.5–6.5, there was a maximum adsorption capacity for UO2 2+. When the pH was less than 5.5, the surface functional groups underwent protonation, leading to a decrease in the adsorption capacity; when the pH was greater than 6.5, UO2 2+ underwent hydrolysis to form precipitates, and thus a decrease in the adsorption capacity occurred. The effect of ionic strength on the adsorption of UO2 2+ by the material was investigated. As shown in Fig. S12c, the material had good salt resistance, and the adsorption capacity of UO2 2+ did not change significantly when the salt concentration was 1 mol/L. Finally, the effect of coexisting ions on the adsorption of UO2 2+ by the material was investigated by comparing the adsorption capacity of the material for UO2 2+ with and without coexisting ions (10 μmol/L). As shown in Fig. S12d, the adsorption capacity of the material for UO2 2+ with and without the coexisting ions was almost unchanged, and the material could be considered to have a good anti-interference ability.

Adsorption kinetic experiments on Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM were conducted to determine the adsorption rate and equilibrium time. Fig. 6 a and b illustrated that the adsorptive capacity gradually rose over time, stabilizing at equilibrium after 100 min. The time for adsorption to reach equilibrium coincided with the stabilization time for fluorescence quenching. Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM exhibited a faster adsorption rate compared to other UO2 2+ adsorbents that required adsorption times ranging from hours to days [31]. The kinetics of adsorption were more accurately represented by the pseudo-second-order model, aligning well with the observed curves, R 2 = 0.988, which strongly proved that the adsorption process was dominated by chemisorption.

In addition, we further evaluated the adsorption performance of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM by adsorption isothermal experiments. The alignment of the adsorption isotherms with the Langmuir model, as depicted in Fig. 6c, suggested that the adsorption occurred uniformly as a monolayer. The maximum theoretical adsorptive capacity of the adsorbent material was calculated to be up to 603 mg/g. Compared to the majority of documented UO2 2+ adsorbents, this material exhibited a markedly greater adsorptive capacity [32].

To comprehensively assess the adsorption performance of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM, comparative experiments were conducted using Tb-MOF, ZIF-8, PAM, Tb-MOF@ZIF-8, and Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM. The results were shown in Fig. S13, although all these materials exhibited some UO2 2+ adsorption capacity, the performance of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM was far superior to the other materials. This excellent performance was primarily due to the material's high hydrophilicity and the specific binding effect of imidazole nitrogen in ZIF-8 and the amino group in PAM on UO2 2+. Additionally, the synergistic interaction between ZIF-8 and PAM contributed significantly to an enhanced adsorption capacity.

Nitrogen adsorption tests were conducted on materials. Based on the obtained adsorption curves, we derived the surface area and pore distribution characteristics for each material. According to the IUPAC classification criteria, the adsorption isotherms of both Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 (Fig. S14) and Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM (Fig. 6d) exhibited type II (or S) characteristics, which indicated that both materials were microporous solids and followed the typical unrestricted single-layer adsorption model. In the isotherms' high-pressure region, the mesoporous hysteresis loops were consistent with type H3, revealed the inhomogeneity of the pore size distribution within both materials. Further, we used the BJH (Barrett-Joiner-Halenda) and H–K (Horvath-Kawazoe) models to analyze the pore size structures of Tb-MOF@ZIF and Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM, and the results showed that both of them contain micropores, mesopores, and macropores. In terms of specific surface area measurements, the specific surface areas of Tb-MOF, ZIF-8, Tb-MOF@ZIF, and Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM were 42.30 m2/g, 3.69 m2/g, 184.17 m2/g, and 2.5 m2/g, respectively. The specific surface area of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 stood out notably in comparison to Tb-MOF and ZIF-8 individually, primarily due to the in-situ growth of ZIF-8 on Tb-MOF's surface. When Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 was embedded in the hydrogel to form the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM complex, its specific surface area decreased.

The swelling ratio of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM was measured. As shown in Fig. S15, the swelling ratios of the material in MES buffer (pH 6) and ultrapure water were 4.19 g/g and 3.63 g/g, respectively. The material exhibited a favorable swelling ratio and high mass transfer efficiency. Additionally, the mechanical strength of the hydrogel composite was tested to evaluate its resistance to mechanical damage. Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM remained intact even when stretched to 200 % of its original length. Furthermore, the composite immediately recovered its original shape after compression, which indicated that Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM possessed excellent mechanical strength (Fig. S16).

The reusability of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM was investigated using an HCl solution. First, the elution time and HCl concentration were optimized. As shown in Fig. S17a–b, UO2 2+ adsorbed in the material were eluted entirely after 30 min of treatment with an HCl solution. The 0.1 mol/L HCl solution exhibited the optimal elution efficiency. Then, the cycling experiments of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM for the adsorption of UO2 2+ were shown in Fig. S18. The adsorption rates of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM composite sphered for UO2 2+ were 97.32 %, 87.87 %, 85.69 %, 84.24 %, and 77.09 % for the five cycles, respectively. The adsorption rate of U(VI) was higher than 80 % until the 4th cycle, which indicated that Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM was an excellent reusable adsorbent for the effective recycling of UO2 2+ from the aqueous matrix.

To explore the detailed mechanism of the adsorption of UO2 2+ by materials, spectroscopic analysis of FT-IR and XPS, in the presence and absence of UO2 2+ adsorption, was conducted, with findings depicted in Fig. 7 a and b. When Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM adsorbed UO2 2+, a novel absorption band was noticeable at 910.96 cm−1 (Fig. 7a), which was attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of OUO [33], indicated that UO2 2+ was successfully adsorbed into the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM material.

In Fig. 7b, the XPS spectra of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 adsorbed and non-adsorbed UO2 2+ were presented. Upon adsorption of UO2 2+ onto Tb-MOF@ZIF-8, two distinct peaks belonging to U 4f (U 4f5/2 (392.04 eV) and U 4f7/2 (381.32 eV)) were observed (Fig. 8 a), suggested that UO2 2+ bound to the surface of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 [34].

To determine the chemical state of elements in Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 pre- and post-uranyl uptake, high-definition XPS data for N 1s, O 1s, C 1s, Tb 3d, and Zn 2p regions were obtained. The high-resolution N 1s spectra of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 exhibited two distinct peaks at 398.88 eV and 399.24 eV, attributable to the -N-Zn and –CN–Zn bonds, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 8b [35]. Upon the adsorption of UO2 2+ by Tb-MOF@ZIF-8, shifts of peaks was observed, with the -N-Zn peak increasing to 398.95 eV and the –CN–Zn peak to 400.07 eV. The shift in binding energy indicated a chemical interaction occurring between nitrogen-rich sites and the UO2 2+, which resulted in a higher binding energy for the N atom upon UO2 2+ adsorption [36].

The high-definition O 1s spectrum of the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 composite, as depicted in Fig. 8c, exhibited two distinct peaks at binding energies of 531.18 eV and 532.87 eV, which corresponded to the Zn–OH in ZIF-8 [35] and -O-CO bond in Tb-MOF [37], respectively, and decreased to 530.71 eV and 531.08 eV when the material combined with UO2 2+. A reduction in binding energy suggested that hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties within the material play a role in the uptake of UO2 2+ [38].

Split-peak fitting of the C 1s of Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 revealed the presence of three peaks at 284.61 eV, 285.9 eV, and 287.82 eV, which corresponded to C–C/C–H/CC, C–O, and C–N/C–N bonds [37], respectively (Fig. 8d). When UO2 2+ were present in the solution, the peaks at 285.9 eV and 287.82 eV shifted to 285.77 eV and 287.82 eV, respectively, while 284.61 eV did not change. This indicated that the nitrogen-containing sites and carboxyl groups in Tb-MOF could participate in the adsorption of UO2 2+.

Fig. 8e and f displayed the high-definition XPS spectra of Tb 3d and Zn 2p, respectively. After fitting them to separate peaks, there were two peaks at 1241.05 and 1276.43 for Tb 3d, which were attributed to Tb 3d5/2 and Tb 3d3/2 [39]. In addition, distinct peaks were observed at 1021.68 and 1044.69 for the Zn 2p spectrum, ascribed to the Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 transitions, respectively [40]. After the absorption of UO2 2+, there was no change in Zn 2p, while there was a decrease in the binding energy and intensity of Tb 3d. The peak intensity of the XPS spectrum was related to the relative amounts of atoms [39]. A decrease in the intensity of the peak of Tb could be assumed to be an ion exchange between Tb3+ and UO2 2+.

Furthermore, it was observed from Fig. 5d that at pH 6, the Zeta potential of the UO2 2+ was positive while the Tb-MOF@ZIF-8 exhibited a negative potential. Therefore, electrostatic interactions were also one of the reasons for the adsorption behavior to proceed.

The adsorption behavior of materials on UO2 2+ was considered as the following mechanisms: (a) coordination of nitrogen-containing sites in Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM with UO2 2+, (b) ligand bonding and hydrogen bonding between UO2 2+ and carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM, (c) ion exchange between UO2 2+ and Tb3+ and electrostatic interactions between the material and UO2 2+.

In summary, a novel Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM hydrogel complex based on Tb-MOF, ZIF-8, and PAM was successfully prepared, and a novel approach facilitating concurrent adsorption and detection of UO2 2+ was devised. Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM had the advantages of sharper and stronger rocket-like characteristic emission peaks, higher fluorescence quantum yield, large Stokes shifts, long fluorescence lifetimes, good thermal stability, salt resistance, and abundant pore structures. Upon employed Tb-MOF@ZIF-8@PAM for concurrent adsorption and detection of UO2 2+, the method demonstrated excellent adsorption capacity, ultra-high detection sensitivity, and outstanding immunity to interference. It was reasonable to believe that this new method possessed significant potential in UO2 2+ detection and removal.
